questions.text: A double bond consists of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: one sigma and one pi bond
options.explanation: A double bond is formed by the combination of one sigma bond, resulting from head-on orbital overlap, and one pi bond, resulting from sideways overlap of p orbitals. This combination provides the additional electron density and restricted rotation characteristic of double bonds.Sigma (σ) Bond:A sigma bond is formed by the end-to-end overlap of two atomic orbitals, allowing for effective head-on collision and sharing of electron density along the axis connecting the nuclei of the bonded atoms. In a double bond, there is one sigma bond, which is typically formed by the overlap of sp3 hybridized orbitals or sp2 hybridized orbitals.Pi (π) Bond:A pi bond is formed by the side-to-side overlap of two parallel p orbitals that are perpendicular to the sigma bond axis. In a double bond, the pi bond is created by the overlap of p orbitals from adjacent carbon atoms. The presence of a pi bond restricts rotation around the sigma bond axis and contributes to the overall stability of the double bond

questions.text: Chemical properties of first member of homologous series with respect to other members are
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While members of a homologous series share some similar properties due to the same functional group and general formula, the first member exhibits some distinct chemical properties compared to its higher homologues. Here's why:Physical properties: The first member generally has weaker intermolecular forces due to its smaller size and fewer van der Waals interactions. This results in lower boiling and melting points compared to higher homologues, which have stronger intermolecular forces due to their increased size and surface area.Reactivity: The first member in some homologous series (especially alkanes) can be less reactive towards certain reactions due to the absence of additional branching or functional groups that activate the molecule. For example, methane (first member of alkanes) is quite unreactive compared to higher alkanes with branching or other functional groups.Acidic/basic properties: In cases where acidity or basicity is affected by carbon chain length, the first member may exhibit differing acidic/basic characteristics compared to higher homologues. For example, the first member of carboxylic acids is generally weaker than its higher homologues due to the electron-withdrawing effect of the additional methylene groups in longer chains

questions.text: The reaction of benzene with bromine gives?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction of benzene with bromine in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst, such as FeBr_3 , results in electrophilic aromatic substitution. The bromine molecule is polarized by the Lewis acid, forming a bromonium ion (Br<sup>+</sup>) which acts as the electrophile. The bromonium ion attacks the benzene ring, leading to the formation of bromobenzene. However, the reaction doesn't stop there.In the presence of a strong base, like NaOH, bromobenzene undergoes further reaction to form bromo-phenol. This reaction involves the nucleophilic attack of hydroxide ion (OH<sup>-</sup>) on the bromobenzene, leading to the formation of bromo-phenol.

questions.text: Benzene is prepared in laboratory by distillation of phenol with
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The preparation of benzene from phenol involves a process called reduction. Zinc dust (Zn) acts as a reducing agent, converting the hydroxyl group (-OH) of phenol into a hydrogen atom (-H). The reaction is typically carried out in the presence of a strong acid, such as concentrated HCl.The overall reaction can be represented as:C_6 H_5 OH + Zn + HCl → C_6 H_6 + ZnCl_2 + H_2 O

questions.text: Which of the following statements is incorrect about benzene?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: All of the carbon atoms are sp hybridized.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The carbon atoms in benzene are sp<sup>2</sup> hybridized, not sp hybridized. Each carbon atom in benzene forms three sigma bonds (two with other carbon atoms and one with a hydrogen atom) using its three sp<sup>2</sup> hybrid orbitals. The remaining unhybridized p orbital on each carbon atom overlaps with the p orbitals of adjacent carbon atoms to form a delocalized pi system above and below the plane of the benzene ring.

options.text: The carbon-carbon bond lengths are all the same
options.explanation: Due to the delocalization of electrons in the pi system, all the carbon-carbon bonds in benzene have an equal bond length, which is intermediate between a single and a double bond.
The six pi electrons in benzene are delocalized over the entire ring, giving it a special stability.

options.explanation: The carbon-hydrogen bonds in benzene are also equal due to the symmetry of the molecule.

options.text: All twelve atoms lie in the same plane
options.explanation: The benzene molecule is planar, with all twelve atoms lying in the same plane.

questions.text: Friedel-Craft's reaction gives?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The Friedel-Crafts reaction is a type of electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction that involves the alkylation or acylation of aromatic compounds.In the case of the alkylation of benzene, the reaction uses an alkyl halide (like CH_3 Cl) and a Lewis acid catalyst (like AlCl_3 ) to introduce an alkyl group onto the benzene ring. The product of this reaction is toluene.

questions.text: Benzene when reacted with chlorine in ultraviolet rays produces:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction of benzene with chlorine in the presence of ultraviolet light is a free radical halogenation reaction. The UV light provides the energy needed to break the chlorine molecule into chlorine radicals. These radicals then attack the benzene ring, leading to the formation of a series of chlorinated products.The primary product of this reaction is hexachlorocyclohexane (HCH), also known as lindane. HCH is a cyclic compound with six chlorine atoms attached to the cyclohexane ring.

questions.text: The hybridization of benzene is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As we discussed before, each carbon atom in benzene forms three sigma bonds (two with other carbon atoms and one with a hydrogen atom). This requires three hybrid orbitals for bonding. To form these three hybrid orbitals, one s orbital and two p orbitals from the carbon atom hybridize, resulting in sp<sup>2</sup> hybridization. The remaining unhybridized p orbital on each carbon atom overlaps with the p orbitals of adjacent carbon atoms to form a delocalized pi system above and below the plane of the benzene ring.

questions.text: Acetylene upon heating at 300 C gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When acetylene (C_2 H_2 ) is heated at 300°C, it undergoes a process called trimerization. This means three acetylene molecules combine to form a new molecule. In this case, the trimerization of acetylene leads to the formation of benzene (C_6 H_6 ).

questions.text: Friedel-Crafts Reaction can be used for the preparation of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As discussed earlier, the Friedel-Crafts reaction is an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction commonly used for the alkylation or acylation of aromatic compounds. One specific example is the alkylation of benzene with methyl chloride (CH_3 Cl) using a Lewis acid catalyst like AlCl_3 , which produces toluene.While the Friedel-Crafts reaction can be used to modify benzene, it's not directly used to prepare benzene itself.
